sentences.text: No, no.
It's all right.
No, it's all right.
Yeah.
There was also a big roller for leveling the ground, which 40 prisoners from this penalty company used to drive.
The big kapo sat on the handle of the roller.
And the prisoners in-- in running condition went all over the place of Auschwitz, where the roll [? call ?] was-- roll up was on and in order to level it.
Whoever couldn't drive anymore or pull the-- the roller was rolled over, fallen down, and he was rolled over.
And he never lived anymore.

Further on, there was also-- what they called-- if somebody run away from camp, 10 inmates, where they lived or worked, were taken to the penalty company where they were given no food and were starved to death.
10 people were starved to death.
On one occasion, the SS, the main Lagerkommandant, pointed out at a fellow that's standing next to me.
I thought he's pointing at me.
And I was nearly going to go out.
And then the-- the commandant pointed again at a man next to me, a bit older, maybe more exhausted.
So I breathed, rather heavily, to-- with joy that it wasn't me.
And he started shouting, oh, no, not me!
But the kapo gave him a hit in the face and pulled him out of the ranks.
And they moved him right away to the penalty company.
They were locked in a special room, cell, and hungered to death.
There was no-- there was-- it wasn't possible, because if you try to give any help, that company was separated, isolated from everybody.
They were mainly Jews and priests.
Later on, priests were moved to Dachau.
They didn't send them to penalty company.
Somebody must have made a claim for it.
I believe it was the-- the King of Italy wife.
I'm not sure.
Spiritually, maybe.
They couldn't-- couldn't help anywhere, because they were in the same position as the other inmates.
There were also people sent by order of the Gestapo.
When there were-- they came to the camp, they had special marking, a red circle, that means nobody could approach them.
They were brought right to the penalty company, and nobody could go in there.
Did you wear any sort of badge?
I had a triangle with a red color.
That meant political prisoner.
Jews used to wear a star of David.
But normally, in the camp, there weren't any Jews till later on, when they used to bring them en masse.
In 1942, they started bringing them.
But for the time we're talking about now, there weren't any Jews in the camp?
There weren't any Jews around the camp.
They were all-- if there were any, in the penalty company.
I was in various blocks.
They used to move us from number four to number 11 and so on.
So I-- there-- there wasn't a permanent block, because they used to bring people every time.
People used to die and they used to move various ranks.
At the beginning of the camp, I was treated as a teenager.
And I thought they going to teach us German and so, in order to bring citizens out of us.
Later on, that all changed, when so many transports used to come.
They moved us out to a working company.
Well, at the beginning, it was very strict.
You had to be very careful how to step.
Later on, as the wave of war moved, and the Germans realized that the war is not going to last to their victory, they relaxed a bit.
And also some of the old SS men who started building the camp were move to fronts, either west front or east front.
So the change, wave changed.
Well, I've seen people being thrown into the latrine, where they were drowned in-- in, you know, the dirt.
And I've seen also people being hung for contact with civilian population or any other reason which they could-- could have-- if they started stealing some stuff from magazines.
In some cases, they used to treat them as saboteurs and hung them.
There was also-- there was also-- one day, I remember, two Polish officers were trying to escape.
The Lagerkommandant brought them into the-- in front of everybody during a roll call.
And gave them-- one received 100 lashes and the other 150.
And then they were sent to the-- to be-- penalty company to be left for dead after not being given any food.
So that was something.
I couldn't understand why the Lagerkommandant gave so many to show that these people were trying to escape, and that's the reason they were beaten.
Many times, when companies used to come back from work, and there were some people who tried to escape, they were shot.
They used to leave-- leave them in front of the camp, so that everybody entering the gate could see six, eight, 10 people lying down who tried to escape.

There were also reasons to see that people were [? looked ?] [? up. ?] And anyone who had gold teeth was taken in the evening to the toilet, their teeth extracted, and killed, because they were making smuggling with gold or whatever.
I can't understand this.
Well, I-- I hadn't seen it, but I've heard the [INAUDIBLE] when I was sleeping, so I knew what happening.
And then, they told me what's happening.
Those who got-- or sometimes the commandant came in the evening-- no, not the commandant, the block--
--elder came into the-- into the-- our room.
And they started telling nice jokes.
Everybody had to laugh and open face.
And that-- that was the main reason.
The other man next to him who written down on those who had gold teeth showing.
And then we knew these people would be next to be killed.
Yeah, to laugh and open their faces.
And everybody was happy.
The-- the block elder is here.
We must be very nice to him.
That's the-- that's the main reason for it.
The only thing we've seen, somebody writing numbers of the people.
Oh, yes.
One day, I was called-- well, I didn't actually bake potatoes, but one of the kapos seen me baking.
And he says, I am definitely see that you are baking potatoes.
And he-- he took my number down.
And the next thing, after a few weeks, I had a roll call.
And they called my number.
So I went, and they sent me to this penalty company.
And the German, who looked very nasty, told me that for baking potatoes, I've got three hours on a stehbunker, that means-- not stehbunker, but hanging on the pillar.
So he took me upstairs on an attic.
The pillar who supported the roof had a hook.
He hung me on there.
And it was so hard for one hour.
I couldn't-- I couldn't stand it, but somehow I managed.
And for the next few-- next day, I told my brother if he can help me and go in my place.
So he went for me one hour, and the next hour, later on was I finished.
I could-- just-- my hands were so-- swollen.
And I couldn't just-- and I had to go to work the next time I came.
So how long were you left hanging there?
One hour each day.
So I was two hours by two-- for two days.
Well, most of it I tried to avoid, because all I could get, now and again, a beating, but once I moved to the-- to the farm, the beating didn't come so often, because I tried to avoid it.
And I had a bit better food.
And I had better living condition altogether.
Longer, I had more experience how to avoid all these mixups.
Oh, yes.
One-- one day, I remember-- block 10 was next-- next to block 11, which was the penalty company.
The windows facing the block number 11, penalty company, were painted over.
But the-- the-- air-- windows which you opened to get air in, the small windows, were slightly open.
So standing on the top roof-- on the top of the bed, which was three tiers, I could see that there was something going on.
Lights were switched on.
And they brought a-- a woman, a man, and two children, and one-- two children.
One-- one of the children was in the woman's arms.
The SS man called Palitzsch stood behind and shot the children first.
First, one child, the one which was in the-- in the mother's arms.
He shot it at the back of the head.
The brains splashed all over their faces.
The people-- the parents stood quietly, moved nothing.
Then he got the child standing next to mother, holding hands, mother and father.
He pulled down the-- the floor.
The child fought not to try to be shot, but he shot it.
And the next thing, he took the-- the wife of the man and the man, by the-- by the neck or-- he shot them through the back of the head.
That was something horrible.
I couldn't manage to look at it.
That was like a family of two children, with two children.
They must have been political prisoners for some reasons.
Palitzsch was the Rapportfuhrer, who used to relate the amount of people to the Lagerkommandant.
He was a-- he was a German, I presume.
I know, later on, we had no news what happened to him after the war.
But he was the one that used to give any penalty to prisoners, beating or whatever.
He used to enjoy himself.
He was a sadist.
Oh, late-- later on, I was given the job as a coach host driver.
And we used to bring various materials from the surrounding area of Auschwitz, for the keep going of the farm.
And also to supply the food for the inmates in-- where we live.
So when I passed the railway station, I've seen-- although we not were allowed to, I passed next to it, ignoring what's happening.
But I could see, what the-- in the corner of my eye, that there was people arrived in a train on the side lighting, railway station.
They were moved into a area.
There was an SS man standing in front who looks like a doctor to me, who started pointing.
There were two rows of people.
And he was to separate some of them, which was in the minority, which were mainly men, and women with children, and pregnant women or children themselves, there used to be put on a separate row.
Later on, they were moved by the SS people to-- I knew the direction, where it was.
It was the crematorium.
The SS men were very, very kind and good, because they were frightened.
If there's about 400 people, and somebody is trying to stir them, this might be quite a commotion.
Sometimes the transport consists of 1,000 people.
So they were so frightened that they never moved a finger at the people.
There were rather trying to be gentlemen and helpful.
A woman with a child, he tried to hold it for her and so on.
But I knew this was only in order to avoid any panic.
But sometimes, you could see a chap that he was saying, where are you taking us?
Are you going to gas us?
Oh, they knew he is there to annoy all the others.
So they just took him out.
Took him so far away, nobody's seen it, and shot him so there was no more-- any disturbance.
Well, as far as I know, they went to the gas chambers.
And they were told that they going to be having a wash, delouse, and so on.
Put your things down.
And they were moved into another room, which they called a washroom, which didn't look like a washroom.
But they made it look like.
They put tops and everything, so that people would think it's a washroom.
In fact, this was the gas chamber.
Once the last people went in, the SS men and-- were-- they had 60 people working as-- as a special command.
These were Jewish people, which they picked up from the transport.
They could speak the language of the transport.
And they could explain to the people that they-- they going only to be washed and then given work.
Leave all your things quietly down.
We'll-- we'll get you out of here.
But once they were already full in the chamber, the-- one SS was with the two-- two or three sonderkommando people.
They locked the-- the door.
And there was a special team of other people who threw the gas, Zyklon-B, through the windows.
And it was instant death.
After that, they-- they were-- after about half an hour, there was ventilation put on to get the last fragment of the gas out.
And the sonderkommando people used to bring them out.
They have to have a look whether they had any gold teeth on, to remove them, any valuables around the neck or whatever, rings and so on.
They removed all these.
And they also sheared the hair of the women.
I believe they used the hair for the special clothing in the German industry.
The things I saw was the transport being selected and moved in.
The rest, I could sometimes see when they-- when I passed, when they were shearing heads off.
But I had to move away, because that wasn't the way-- my way to go through.
So main thing I've seen is the selection and sending to the gas chambers.
This area was out of bound.
Well, we knew them-- they were gas chambers because the-- when, one day, when there were no-- no people to be gassed, the place was open.
I had-- had a chance to go inside and have a look, what it looked like.
So I'd seen there was an imitation of washroom.
And I've seen where they were undressing.
So I knew that's where it happened.
I could still smell the gas.
But to be quite frank, I hadn't seen them being gassed.
I was aware of some experiments on women where they were trying to put some injections on them.
I'm-- but I'm not sure what they were for.
I was a bit worried, because I-- I knew sometimes they were selecting twins.
But we tried to move when we were in roll-- roll calls, a bit away from each other, so nobody could see right away we are twins.
So that's the way we managed to escape this thing.
Well, they-- they-- people had work done.
They didn't bother about it.
It's just the-- the doctor used to sometimes make roll calls to find him some material for his work.
But otherwise, nobody knew, except we looked alike.
Oh, we did look very much alike, yeah.
We could-- could have been taken, but somehow we managed to get-- get away with it.
The final stay, I was moved to Buchenwald, where-- that was in 1944, somewhere in June or August.
We were moved.
And--
Because the front was coming nearer and nearer.
We knew that the tide-- wave of tide for victories is going to be to our side.

And we knew that the sooner we go, the better, because when they move people in 1944-- at the end of 1944, winter, I seen in Buchenwald, they brought transport people frozen to death in the cattle wagons.
So when I went in summertime, I thought I did the best choice to move then.
Because I could still avoid it if I wanted to, to ask to stay at Auschwitz.
But I didn't, because I knew the end will be worse than that.
Well, I had a commando where we had also Jews, Jewish people, which were sent to-- to work.
They were working as brick layers and so on.
There were some Jews, which were selected to do the work.
When they were exhausted and died, they were sent to-- to be burned in crematorium.
But some of the Jews were working in our command.
No.
Well, the Jewish people were rather worse than the other people, because the Polish people, if they had family, they had a chance to get a packet of food or whatever.
Jewish people had no chance to get from nowhere.
And that's why I was always sorry for them.
If I could sometimes give them a scrap, which I could afford to give, I give.
But otherwise, they were, in my opinion, worse than anybody else, because they were Jews.
By cattle-- by train, we were moved to Buchenwald.
And we knew this won't be for long.
I know Buchenwald was bombed.
One-- one day American Air Force came and bombed all the surrounding workplaces of Buchenwald.
And I often thought, why didn't they do that in Auschwitz?
Send Air Force and bomb all the bunkers which were-- and crematoria, which were outside Auschwitz?
And very often, when after the war, I spoke to some Jewish people, and they said to me, well, nobody was informing us?
Maybe we were making too much business to bother about this.
And that's how it goes.
They must have-- somebody must have been making business.
Well, there was-- there wasn't-- it was only a day journey.
And we received some bread for one day's supply.
And we were packed 40 people.
It was tight, but for that short journey, it wasn't too bad.
When the train got to Buchenwald, we were moved to a separate camp, where they tried to sort out where are we going to go and where we're going to work.
And it was still a bit better, Buchenwald, in that respect.
It was an older camp than Auschwitz.
And it was-- there were a lot of political prisoners, Communists, which ruled the camp.
With it being an established camp, there were no gas chambers as far as I know.
So it was a lot different.
And the tide of war was so near, that the spirit of everybody was much different.
Yeah.
When it came the next-- next few days, we've seen the cannons being shot and so on, we knew that the end is near.
But I don't think we were worried whether they might shoot us before they moved.
But somehow, that didn't happen.
Because we heard that the SS were very keen on destroying people before they capitulate.
So that's why we were a bit worried.
And the camp managed to get some weapon inside the camp to wait for that time when the American did not arrive, that we were not shot.
And also, the guards moved in the camp, were, most of them, Ukrainians, which collaborated with Germans.
So the SS, more or less, weren't so much in-- in the visibility as the Ukrainian guards.
Well, all I did hear is every morning, the Rapportfuhrer, the one who made the appels, used to shout through a microphone, all SS men must move from the camp outside.
Every time that was repeated, because sometimes the SS used to wander about.
So that is the only thing I do remember, that they tried to get them out of the camp.
One year, approximately, maybe three quarter of a year.
Well, we've seen the American arrive.
And they try to give everybody chocolate and friendly.
We moved right away outside the German blocks where the SS were accommodated to see if we can find any food or whatever.
I remember I found some wine there, which I took.
And that's as far as I know.
And they improved the food.
They gave us a lot better food, since the American came, which was rather disastrous.

Because, when you were fed for three or so many years with a very thin food, when you got fatty soup and so on, a lot of people did die from stomach troubles, because the stomach couldn't stand that much food all-- all-- all the sudden.
So that was one of the things which I thought the Americans didn't do right.
My brother was also in Buchenwald, and my twin brother was moved to different camp, which was in-- later on occupied by the Russians.
And he returned back to Poland, because being occupied by Russian, you had no other choice.
My father was-- came to Auschwitz about 1941.
He was put there because he belonged to a-- a underground organization of officers.
And he was moved to Auschwitz, and later on, transferred to-- to Birkenau.
And unfortunately, I received a letter from my mother later on that your father is dead.
He died in Birkenau.
That's as far as I knew about my father.
I've seen him for six months in Auschwitz main camp, but later on, he was moved, which I couldn't stop.
Oh, yes.
We-- we came very often in the evening.
We visited him and inquired how things in Poland and so on.
So we were in contact.
Even my older brother, who had a better job, he managed to give some bread to my father.
But when they moved him to Birkenau, we lost contact completely.
He died probably from exhaustion.
He wasn't shot.
He died from the exhaustion, which a lot of people did, due to lack of food.
American forces.
But what army and so on, I don't-- do not know.
Well, I wasn't actually speaking to any of them.
But I know-- I know a lot of people, especially Jewish, and found a lot of Jewish people in the American army.
And they started shaking hands and kissing each other.
So I knew they were happy that the end of the trauma is over.
After that, I was-- the American commander came one day to their camp.
And they ask if any of the survivors of the camp wished to do a bit of work.
And I said, why not?
I feel all right.
So I asked, what kind of job is that?
Oh, they said, where the American soldiers are accommodated, we want some housekeepers, that means cleaning the floor or do a bit of kitchen work.
So I said, I'm not worried about that.
You get paid wages.
And so I was very happy to go there.
Some Jewish people went with me.
The only thing is, they got the job in the kitchen.
They thought they would be fed better.
But later on, they pitied that, because they had a lot of work, and I swept the floor one morning, and I've not else to do, except relax.
So they were asking, can we change over?
I said, you wanted that job in the kitchen, you stay, mate.
[LAUGHS] And how long did you stay there?
I stayed for about six months.
But they-- they moved the forces later on somewhere else.
So they had-- they had to move somewhere.
So I came back to join the other Polish forces to look after the German-- American magazines of food or whatever.
Where we used to guard them.
Sometimes were looked after the American colonel who slept-- had a special accommodation and needed guards so nobody can give him any trouble.
Well, later on, I-- I moved to-- I heard of my brother being in Italy, joined the Polish forces.
And my older brother, who came later on, that they are in the Polish army in Italy.
So I made contact with them, moved across the border, and joined the Polish forces.
When there's sunshine, the dog is always there.
